title: Revelation 18
intro: The chapter where Bohr's **distinctive contribution** to Adventist Revelation exposition is concentrated: his "Demise of Babylonian Capitalism" thesis. Mostly Rule-compliant; the main exegetical divergence from the DAR pioneer reading is at **v. 2** (Bohr collapses Smith's two-stage Rev 14:8 / Rev 18:2 framing into a single terminal moral collapse). The previously listed v. 13 divergence is withdrawn after DAR audit — Smith himself spiritualizes "slaves and souls of men" at DAR 676.2, so Bohr's body/soul reading is in the same hermeneutic register as DAR, not in violation of it. Source: Bohr, _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, Lesson 7 (pp. 111-143).

verses.text: And after these things I saw another angel come down from heaven, having great power; and the earth was lightened with his glory.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads the descending angel as a fifth eschatological messenger uniting his voice with the three angels of Rev 14 to swell the closing work into a loud cry: “I saw another mighty angel commissioned to descend to the earth, to unite his voice with the third angel, and give power and force to his message. Great power and glory were imparted to the angel, and as he descended, the earth was lightened with his glory” (EW 277.1). She glosses the message identically with Smith’s framing — “this scripture points forward to a time when the announcement of the fall of Babylon, as made by the second angel of Revelation 14 (verse 8), is to be repeated, with the additional mention of the corruptions which have been entering the various organizations that constitute Babylon, since that message was first given, in the summer of 1844” (GC 603.1-603.2). She explicitly identifies the “glory” as the unmasking work of the third angel’s message: “the sins of Babylon will be laid open. The fearful results of enforcing the observances of the church by civil authority, the inroads of spiritualism, the stealthy but rapid progress of the papal power — all will be unmasked” (GC 606.2). The “two calls to the churches” (7BC 985.4) are explicitly the same message given by Rev 14:8.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 666.5; 672.1; specific comment within block at DAR 672.1 on v.1
pioneerReadings.html: Smith establishes the chronology by ruling out a pre-1844 application — "this branch of Babylon, experienced a moral fall by the rejection of the first message of chapter 14" — and concluding: "the first two verses of this chapter constitute **a feature of the third message** which is to appear when this message shall be proclaimed with power, and the whole earth be lightened with its glory" (DAR 666.5-667.1). On the angel itself: "this angel, instead of simply flying 'in the midst of heaven,' like the others, is said to 'come down from heaven.' He comes, as it were, nearer to the earth, with a message more pointed and direct; and he has 'great power,' and the earth is 'lightened with his glory.' **No such description of a message from heaven to man is elsewhere to be found in all the Bible.** This is the last; and as is meet, it comes with surpassing glory and unwonted power" (DAR 672.1). The angel is the fourth of five celestial messengers in the final reformation (the three of Rev 14 + this one + the "voice" of v.4) (DAR 671.3).

Miller’s reading of Rev 18:1 is idiosyncratic and would not survive into mature Adventist exegesis: he identifies the “angel come down from heaven, having great power” not as a symbolic messenger but as Christ Himself descending to take the kingdom. “On turning to Revelation 18:1, 2, we find the angel that follows, crying Babylon is fallen, is one that comes down from heaven, having great power, and lightens the earth with his glory. This angel that follows must therefore be the Lord Jesus Christ descending from heaven to take the kingdom; and when he takes to himself his great power to reign, Satan is no longer the god of this world. It may then be well said, Babylon is fallen, i.e. it has lost the supremacy, Christ has taken that” (WMAD 30.3). For Miller, Rev 18:1 is therefore the Second Advent itself, and the v.4 call to come out is the resurrection summons to meet Christ in the air. The later pioneer reading (Smith, J. White, EGW, Andrews, Haskell) corrects Miller decisively here, identifying the angel as the symbolic latter-rain Loud Cry preceding the plagues, not Christ’s personal coming.

James White treats Rev 18:1 within his architectural defense of the three angels’ messages as a sequenced chain: the angel of Rev 14:6 (the second-advent message, 1840-1843), then Rev 14:8 = Rev 18:1-2 (the fall of Babylon, 1844), then Rev 14:9-12 = Rev 18:4 (the third angel’s message and call out, ongoing since 1844). Verbatim: “If the first position which is so generally taken, is correct, then the other angels’ cries certainly represent so many distinct messages to be proclaimed to God’s people in this mortal state” (TTAM 1.1). The angel of Rev 18:1 is for White the same messenger-class as the angel of Rev 14:8 returning with amplified force; “every advent believer knows that we heard just this message” (TTAM 4.1) — meaning the historical Millerite movement of 1844 was the initial wave, with a future swelling to the loud cry still ahead under the third message.

Andrews concentrates Rev 18:1 within his case for a future (post-1844) fulfillment swelling of the second angel’s message: “In Revelation 18 the message announcing the fall of Babylon is presented again, with additions, showing, as we understand, that there is in the future a mighty movement to take place on this subject. We have no doubt that God has many dear saints united with the various bodies of professed Christians. Those, we believe, will yet hear the call given in Revelation 18:4” (TMR 61.2). On the angel’s function: “for us to be able to leave Babylon, it is necessary that the angel of God should illuminate the earth with his glory (Revelation 18:1, 2), and thus dissipate the darkness which its errors have caused. This angel accomplishes his work in intimate connection with the angel who announces that the hour of God’s Judgment is come, and with that other angel who preaches the commandments of God and the faith of Jesus” (TMR 71.2). Andrews therefore reads Rev 18:1 as the necessary illumination prerequisite for the v.4 call — the latter rain that lets God’s people see Babylon clearly enough to leave her.

Haskell treats Rev 18 as the historicist climax of the church-state apostasy of Rev 17, with the v.1 angel as the agent of Babylon’s unmasking just before the plagues: “the sins of the ancient city are repeated by the last church, and its overthrow is the object lesson, to the world, of the final destruction of the whole world when Christ comes down, because her iniquity has reached heaven. The figure is followed throughout the eighteenth chapter of Revelation; and by comparing scripture with scripture, the grievous sins of modern Babylon stand out in such awful distinctness that they justify the judgments of God as meted out in the plagues. Such a study opens the mind to the meaning of the cry of the mighty angel, referred to in verses one and two” (SSP 301.2). Haskell anchors the Rev 18 message in the Babel-typology (Gen 11) — ancient Babel’s confusion reaches its eschatological completion in modern Babylon, and the angel of v.1 is sent to expose that completion so the people of God can flee.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 129
modernReadings.html: The Loud Cry angel — not a distinct fourth angel, but the amplification of the three angels' messages of Rev 14; "glory" = the truth about God's character of love as revealed in His Law. Verbatim: "This is the Loud Cry angel. He is not the same Angel that calls the faithful out of Babylon in verse 4" (p. 129).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule IV (correctly cross-linking Rev 14 three-angels and Rev 18:1) and Rule V (citing EGW's exposition of Rev 18:1 as the three-fold message amplified).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.1)
notes.html: The "Loud Cry" label and the cross-citations to _Early Writings_ p. 277 and _Great Controversy_ p. 603 are standard pioneer/EGW vocabulary, but Smith's v.1 paragraph itself does not use the phrase "Loud Cry"; he calls it "this last" message of "surpassing glory and unwonted power." The "glory = truth about God's character" gloss is not in Smith's v.1 paragraph; Smith's emphasis falls on the proximity and intensity of the message, not on its theological content as "character."

verses.text: And he cried mightily with a strong voice, saying, Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen, and is become the habitation of devils, and the hold of every foul spirit, and a cage of every unclean and hateful bird.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW unambiguously holds the two-stage fall framework that Bohr collapses. The Rev 14:8 announcement in 1844 began the moral fall by the rejection of the first message; Rev 18:2 is the future, intensified republication of that fall — “a terrible condition of the religious world is here described. With every rejection of truth the minds of the people will become darker, their hearts more stubborn, until they are entrenched in an infidel hardihood... As the teachings of spiritualism are accepted by the churches, the restraint imposed upon the carnal heart is removed... A belief in spiritual manifestations opens the door to seducing spirits and doctrines of devils, and thus the influence of evil angels will be felt in the churches” (GC 603.2-604.1). EGW thus pinpoints spiritualism in the churches as the concrete mechanism completing Babylon’s fall — the same anchor Smith uses at DAR 667.1. On the two-fold “is fallen”: “[Rev 18:1, 2 quoted.] This is the same message that was given by the second angel. Babylon is fallen, ‘because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.’ What is that wine? — her false doctrines. She has given to the world a false sabbath instead of the Sabbath of the fourth commandment, and has repeated the falsehood that Satan first told to Eve in Eden — the natural immortality of the soul” (7BC 985.4). Two stages, one identifying mechanism: false sabbath + natural immortality of the soul.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 663.2-664.1; 666.5; 667.1; specific comment within block at DAR 666.5 and 667.1 on v.2
pioneerReadings.html: Smith does carry a two-stage reading, but his stages are framed as **(1) the Rev 14:8 fall** (announced from 1844 onward, the moral fall that the Millerite movement first proclaimed) and **(2) the Rev 18:2 fall** ("either synchronous with the message of the fall of Babylon, in chapter 14, or it is given at a later period than that. But it cannot be synonymous with that; for that merely announces the fall of Babylon, while this adds several particulars which at that time were neither fulfilled nor in process of fulfilment... this message is yet future. But we are now having the third angel's message... we are therefore held to the conclusion that the first two verses of this chapter constitute a feature of the third message which is to appear when this message shall be proclaimed with power" DAR 666.5-667.1). On the mechanism completing the demonic possession: "The work brought to view in verse 2 is in process of accomplishment, and will soon be completed, by the work of **Spiritualism**. What are called in Revelation 16:14 'spirits of devils working miracles' are secretly but rapidly working their way into the religious denominations" (DAR 667.1). The "habitation of devils" is therefore concretely Spiritualism's infiltration of fallen Protestantism, not generic demonic imagery.

Miller’s reading concentrates the “Babylon is fallen” identification on Rev 14:8 (which he reads as the 1798 fall of the papal civil power) and treats Rev 18:1-2 as a separate, end-time, Christ-Himself-descending event. “Verse 8: ‘And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.’ This verse shows the downfall of the papal power, or mystical Babylon, which was fulfilled in 1798, when she lost her power to rule over the kings of the earth” (MWV1 136.1). In WMAD he explicitly defends a two-stage reading: “Babylon is fallen, i.e. it has lost the supremacy, Christ has taken that; but while it has fallen it is not destroyed: before that can be done, the saints must be taken out, that they may not partake of the consequences of her sins, nor receive of her plagues” (WMAD 30.3). Note: Miller’s “Babylon” here is papal Rome only; the later pioneer move (J. White, Bates, Smith, EGW) expanding Babylon to include fallen Protestantism was a development post-1844 that Miller did not himself make.

J. White treats Rev 18:2 as the second angel’s message historically fulfilled in 1844 by the Millerite call to leave the apostate churches: “the way was fully prepared for the second message — ‘Babylon is fallen, is fallen,’ ‘Come out of her my people.’ Every advent believer knows that we heard just this message... we have not forgotten the effect that it had upon God’s people, for they obeyed the message and with haste left the churches. This prophecy was exactly fulfilled, and in the right time, and place. Some tell us that Babylon here, is the Roman Catholic church; but God’s people were not in that church. The first message was to the churches, from about 1840 to 1843; and the second angel ‘followed,’ therefore the message — ‘Babylon is fallen’ — ‘Come out of her my people’ was in 1844” (TTAM 4.1). White explicitly rejects the Catholic-only reading and identifies Babylon as the Protestant denominations that rejected the advent message — the same identification Smith carries forward.

Andrews systematically argues the two-stage fall and identifies the “habitation of devils” with spiritualism in the churches. On the chronology: “the cry, ‘Come out of her, my people,’ is made after the announcement that she has fallen. Revelation 18:2, 4. It is therefore evident that Babylon exists after her fall, and that the people of God are still in her midst; therefore her fall must be distinct from her destruction” (TMR 49.2). On the demonic-possession imagery: “Babylon becomes the hold of every foul spirit and the cage of every unclean and hateful bird in consequence of her fall... It would be absurd to represent this as the consequence of her loss of civil power” (TMR 50.5). And on present fulfillment: “it is manifest that Babylon is rapidly becoming the hold of foul spirits, and of unclean and hateful birds. The call to come out of her is made while her plagues are immediately impending” (TMR 62.1). Andrews subdivides Babylon into three sections (Catholic / Greek / Protestant per Rev 16:19) and argues that the fall is announced only after the third — Protestant — section has fallen (TMR 71.1). His framework is the standard pioneer two-stage reading and pre-Bohr exegetical default.

Haskell ties the “habitation of devils” specifically to the threefold confederacy of paganism, papacy, and apostate Protestantism animated by Spiritualism just before the plagues: “The beast and his image seek to control all nations. Satan works in a way never before known... The miracle-working power of Spiritualism adds strength to the oppression. Paganism (the dragon), the papacy (the beast), and fallen Protestantism (the false prophet), join hands. Urged on by the unclean spirits, deadly decrees are issued by this threefold union, and Satan himself appears in person” (SSP 283.2). On v.2 directly: “by comparing scripture with scripture, the grievous sins of modern Babylon stand out in such awful distinctness that they justify the judgments of God as meted out in the plagues. Such a study opens the mind to the meaning of the cry of the mighty angel, referred to in verses one and two” (SSP 301.2). Haskell anchors the imagery typologically in literal Babel/Babylon (Gen 11; Isa 47), and reads “is fallen, is fallen” as terminal moral collapse after the 1844 first announcement — the standard pioneer two-stage frame.

Jones applies Rev 18:2 to the American religious-political situation: when National Reformers succeed in disfranchising conscientious dissenters from civic life, “the professed Christian churches of the country would become, in fact, that which the Revelation has shown in prophecy, ‘a hold of every foul spirit, and a cage of every unclean and hateful bird.’ Revelation 18:2” (TTR 706.4). In ECE he develops the same point structurally: “the apostasy of Protestantism exalts the papacy... once more she guides and dominates the nations. Consequently she glorifies herself and lives deliciously; the kings of the earth commit fornication and live deliciously with her... and therefore she congratulates herself, saying in her heart, ‘I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow.’” (ECE 873.1). For Jones the “habitation of devils” is the moral consequence of Protestantism trading its dissenting principle for civil power — the same Spiritualism-as-mechanism reading that Smith and EGW give, here put in the political-theological vocabulary of Two Republics.

Bates, who lived through the 1844 fulfillment, gives the most detailed first-hand exposition of v.2 as historically inaugurated in 1844: “this way mark was now distinctly seen in our pathway, linked in with, or chained on to the judgment hour cry, and stretching its way through the tarrying time, crying mightily with a strong voice [as was never heard before, nor since Oct. 1844], ‘Babylon is fallen, is fallen, and is become [not will be] the habitation of devils, and the hold of every foul spirit, and a cage of every unclean and hateful bird’” (BP2 65.1). Bates is emphatic that “is become” locks the fall in present-tense perfect from 1844 forward, not in a future-only sense. He defends this against Miller’s own two-stage-but-future framing: “Mr. Miller now came out with a different exposition, showing that Babylon would fall twice — first on the French Revolution, in the eighteenth century, second at the final destruction of all things. It was clearly seen that this position, if followed, would preclude the necessity of the 2nd and 3rd messages that were to follow the first” (BP2 60.1). Bates identifies Babylon as “the existing organized churches which had now fallen in consequence of rejecting the doctrine of the Second Advent” (BP2 62.4) — the canonical post-1844 pioneer move.

Fitch’s “Come Out of Her My People” sermon (1843) is the urtext of Adventist Rev 18:2-4 exegesis, but the surviving text in the local DB is the March 1844 Midnight Cry letter announcing his personal compliance with that call: “our dear Brother Storrs says that the Great Head of the Church designed that we should come out of Babylon, and not wait for Babylon to thrust us out. I believe he is right, and I am therefore determined to come out” (LCFMC 1.1). He then formalizes his ecclesiastical exit: “I do from this time regard myself, and hereby proclaim myself to all men, as free and independent of all ecclesiastical domination, as a member of no sect, and a subscriber to no creed. At the same time, I received the Bible as the Word of the Living God, and am looking for the immediate coming of the Lord Jesus Christ” (LCFMC 1.3). Fitch’s lived identification of Babylon’s “fall” with the Protestant denominations that rejected the advent message (specifically the Newark Presbytery and the Congregational Association) is the practical exegesis behind the second angel’s message — he treats “Babylon is fallen” not as theoretical doctrine but as a personal call to leave.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 130
modernReadings.html: The double "is fallen" = Hebrew synonymous parallelism / crescendo for emphasis, not two chronological falls; Babylon's moral fall is now irreversible just before probation closes. Verbatim: "In Hebrew poetry this is what is called synonymous parallelism. At this point, just before the close of probation, Babylon will have experienced an irreversible moral fall that is beyond repair" (p. 130). Bohr does not distinguish first-fall (1844, moral) from second-fall (end-time) — the standard pioneer two-fold reading.

items.body: The pioneer two-fold-fall reading harmonizes Rev 14:8's _first_ announcement (1844 onward, the moral/doctrinal fall recognized by the Millerite movement) with Rev 18:2's _second_ announcement (end-time, when the political and economic collapse becomes complete). This is the standard EGW reading (_GC_ 603-604: "The message of Revelation 14, announcing the fall of Babylon, must apply to religious bodies that were once pure and have become corrupt. Since this message follows the warning of the judgment, it must be given in the last days; therefore it cannot refer to the Roman Church alone, for that church has been in a fallen condition for many centuries... Not yet, however, can it be said that Babylon is fallen... A more dreadful condition is to come."). Bohr collapses this two-stage chronology into one terminal moment — losing Rule IV's "bring all scriptures together" balance.

The two-fold "is fallen, is fallen" carries chronological weight in the pioneer reading; Bohr treats it as decorative repetition. Hebrew synonymous parallelism is a real literary feature — but it does not exclude chronological doubling, especially when paralleled by an _earlier_ announcement of the same fall (Rev 14:8) that historically anchored the 1844 movement.

status.label: Miller-rule status (the rest of the verse)
status.html: No violation on the demonic-possession imagery (Rule XII trace to Isa 13:21-22 and Jer 50:39 — the literal Babylon's prophesied haunted-ruin state).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.2)
notes.html: The Isa 13:21-22 / Jer 50:39 "haunted-ruin" type/antitype trace is standard pioneer typology but is not invoked in Smith's v.2 paragraph; Smith roots the demonic-possession imagery in Spiritualism (Rev 16:14), not in the OT literal-Babylon ruins. The _GC_ 603-604 quote is EGW corroboration, not from DAR; Smith does not himself use the Hebrew synonymous-parallelism vs chronological-doubling categories — he simply treats Rev 14:8 and Rev 18:2 as two messages at two times.

verses.text: For all nations have drunk of the wine of the wrath of her fornication, and the kings of the earth have committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth are waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW identifies the “wine” doctrinally and the “merchants” ecclesiastically: “Babylon is fallen, ‘because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.’ What is that wine? — her false doctrines. She has given to the world a false sabbath instead of the Sabbath of the fourth commandment, and has repeated the falsehood that Satan first told to Eve in Eden — the natural immortality of the soul. Many kindred errors she has spread far and wide” (7BC 985.4). On the “all nations have drunk” charge specifically: “God denounces Babylon ‘because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.’ This means that she has disregarded the only commandment which points out the true God, and has torn down the Sabbath, God’s memorial of creation” (7BC 979.5). EGW’s reading is therefore doctrinal/sabbatarian where Smith’s is moralistic-ecclesial (Sunday-keeping worldly luxury) and Bohr’s is economic; the three readings are complementary layers of the same indictment.

Smith's whole v.3 commentary is a direct indictment of **luxurious worldly church-members**: "Verse 3 shows the wide extent of the influence of Babylon, and the evil that has resulted and will result from her course, and hence the justness of her punishment. The merchants of the earth are waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies. Who take the lead in all the extravagances of the age? Who load their tables with the richest and choicest viands? Who are foremost in extravagance in dress, and all costly attire? Who are the very personification of pride and arrogance? — **Are they not church-members?** Where shall we look for the very highest exhibition of the luxury, vain show, and pride of life, resulting from the vanity and sin of the race? — **Is it not to a modern church assembly on a pleasant Sunday?**" (DAR 670.2). For Smith, the "merchants made rich by her delicacies" reading is moralistic-ecclesial (Sunday-keeping worldly Christians enriched by Babylon's luxury culture), not narrowly commercial.

Andrews introduces his exposition of the second angel by quoting verse 3 directly: "And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city because she made all nations drink of the wine of wrath of her fornication" (TMR 36.1). He treats verse 3 as the divine warrant for the second angel's proclamation: the cause of Babylon's fall is her successful propagation of false doctrine across all nations. The "wine" is, on Andrews's reading, the corrupt teaching of the apostate religious system; the "fornication" is her unlawful union with the kings of the earth (cf. TMR 38.2). The merchants' enrichment in the same verse is the historical fact that ecclesiastical alliance with civil power generates wealth for the religious-commercial class — a system Andrews sees flowering in nineteenth-century America under the slave-power-church alliance (TMR 45.1).

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 132
modernReadings.html: Economic power is Babylon's defining attribute; the Greek translated "luxury" is _dúnamis_ ("power"), and now Babylon — who forbade the saints to buy or sell (Rev 13:15) — is herself bankrupt. Verbatim: "The power of Babylon resided in her economic clout. Now she is going to totally lose her power because she will no longer be able to sell her merchandise" (p. 132).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule XII (linking _dúnamis_ through other NT contexts) and Rule IV (correctly cross-linking Rev 18:3 with Rev 13:17 on the buy/sell economic system).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.3)
notes.html: The three-fold structural parsing (nations / kings / merchants) and the Jer 51:7 cross-link are standard pioneer commentary moves but are not in Smith's v.3 paragraph. Smith does not unpack a chiastic complicity-then-lament architecture; he reads the verse as straightforward moral indictment of luxury-loving professed Christians inside Babylon.

verses.text: And I heard another voice from heaven, saying, Come out of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW makes Rev 18:4 the central call of the eschatological remnant message: “God still has a people in Babylon; and before the visitation of His judgments these faithful ones must be called out, that they partake not of her sins and ‘receive not of her plagues.’ Hence the movement symbolized by the angel coming down from heaven, lightening the earth with his glory and crying mightily with a strong voice, announcing the sins of Babylon. In connection with his message the call is heard: ‘Come out of her, My people.’ These announcements, uniting with the third angel’s message, constitute the final warning to be given to the inhabitants of the earth” (GC 604.1). The Early Writings vision dramatizes the actual call-out: “the light that was shed upon the waiting ones penetrated everywhere, and those in the churches who had any light, who had not heard and rejected the three messages, obeyed the call and left the fallen churches... the precious were hurried out of the doomed churches, as Lot was hurried out of Sodom before her destruction” (EW 277.2-278.2). EGW’s OT-type chain for the call-out is therefore Lot/Sodom (Gen 19), not Isa 48/Jer 50-51 explicitly, but in the same typological register.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 671.1; 671.3-673.1; specific comment within vv.4-8 block at DAR 671.3-672.1 on v.4
pioneerReadings.html: Smith identifies the "voice from heaven" as a **fifth celestial messenger** added to the three angels of Rev 14 and the angel of Rev 18:1: "This voice from heaven is called 'another' voice, showing that a new agency is here introduced. We now have five celestial messengers expressly mentioned as engaged in this last religious reformation. These are the first, second, and third angels of chapter 14; fourth, the angel of verse one of this chapter; and fifth, the agency indicated by the 'voice' of verse 4" (DAR 671.3). On the dramatic moment of the call: "Suddenly another voice rings out from heaven, 'Come out of her, my people!' **The humble, sincere, devoted children of God, of whom there are some still left, and who sigh and cry over the abominations done in the land**, heed the voice, wash their hands of her sins, separate from her communion, escape, and are saved, while Babylon becomes the victim of the just judgments of God" (DAR 673.0). The remnant inside Babylon is concrete: "for God has still a people there, and she must be entitled to some regard on their account until they are called from her communion" (DAR 671.1). Smith cross-links to Rev 14:4 (144,000 not defiled with women) at DAR 673.1.

Miller’s reading of Rev 18:4 is again idiosyncratic: he identifies the “come out of her” call not as a separation from apostate Christianity but as the resurrection/translation summons at the Second Advent itself. “The time has then come when the dead are to be raised; and this must synchronize with the voice from heaven in Revelation 18:4, saying, ‘Come out of her, my people.’ To come, is to go in the direction of the invitation. It must therefore be the invitation from where Christ will then have come to meet him in the air” (WMAD 30.3). For Miller the call-out is a literal vertical ascent at the Parousia, not a pre-plague horizontal separation from the fallen churches. This is the reading that Bates (BP2 60.1-62.1), Andrews (TMR 49.2, 62.2), J. White (TTAM 4.1) and EGW (GC 604.1) all explicitly correct — the standard pioneer reading requires the call to be a pre-plague separation, not the rapture itself.

J. White makes Rev 18:4 the operative call that actually brought the post-1844 Sabbatarian Adventist movement into existence: “the second angel’s message reached to the fall of 1844, where the cry — ‘Come out of her my people’ closed: then the time for the third came... we know also that the time for keeping all the commandments right has been since 1844, since God called us out of Babylon. If we had stayed there, bound down by ministers and creeds, the glorious light of the Holy Sabbath never would have reached us, but glory to God, the second angel’s message called us out from the fallen churches where we are now free to think, and act for ourselves in the fear of God” (TTAM 11.3). For White, Rev 18:4 is causally connected to the discovery of the Sabbath truth: only those who obeyed the call out of Babylon could receive the third angel’s commandment-keeping message. This is the foundational pioneer ecclesiology for SDA self-identity.

Andrews systematically grounds the chronology of v.4 against post-millennial readings: “the destruction of Babylon, as described in Revelation 18, takes place under the seventh vial; for it is under that vial that she comes in remembrance before God, to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath. Revelation 16:17-21. The people of God are called out of her just before the seven last plagues are poured out. Those plagues are future, as we shall hereafter prove. Hence it is certain that Revelation 18 cannot be applied to the events of the sixteenth century” (TMR 62.2). On the necessity of the call going forward into the future: “at the time of these messages... the people of God are called out of Babylon, — a proof conclusive that the greater part are in Babylon when this proclamation is made” (TMR 71.2). Andrews’ argument forecloses both the Catholic-only and the past-only readings: the call is given at the threshold of the seventh plague, to a remnant still inside the fallen Protestant churches.

Haskell types the call out of Babylon directly on the angels hurrying Lot out of Sodom — the same OT-type EGW uses at EW 278: “there will be a voice heard from heaven saying, ‘Come out of her, My people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues. For her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities.’ As the angels took Lot by the hands, and hurried him out of Sodom, bidding him not to look back, so angels will hurry the sincere and true-hearted out of Babylon, for her destruction cometh like the burning of Sodom” (SSP 311.1). On the timing and theological force: “as the ancient city of Babylon was overthrown because she forsook the way of life, so modern Babylon dies. None need partake of her plagues; for all had an opportunity to separate from her midst. God is to-day making up His spiritual kingdom” (SSP 313.1). Haskell adds nothing to Smith’s framework but reinforces the angelic-agency reading by typing the angels of Rev 18:4 onto the angels of Gen 19.

Waggoner ties Rev 18:4 to the Jeremiah 25 punishment-of-Babylon prophecy and reads the call as the gospel summons for the dispersed people of God in every age — with eschatological intensification in the last days: “that the deliverance of Israel is not a mere local affair, is plainly shown in the punishment threatened upon Babylon, in the twenty-fifth chapter of Jeremiah. It was at the close of the seventy years’ captivity that God purposed to bring this punishment; but, as we have already seen, Israel was not fully ready to be gathered at that time. From that day to this, many of God’s people have been in Babylon, so that the word comes in these latter days, as well as then, ‘Come out of her My people.’ Jeremiah 2:4, 5; Revelation 18:4” (EVCO 520.1). Waggoner’s reading harmonizes with Bates’ literal-Babylon/spiritual-Babylon typology but extends the call backward to the OT remnant in Babylon — the eschatological “come out” is the climactic instance of a perennial covenantal pattern, not a unique end-time event in isolation.

Bates gives the most extensive defense of v.4 as ecclesiastical-separation message addressed to the organized Protestant churches: “the third angel followed them, saying, with a loud voice, if any man worship the beast and his image... and is in substance the same as chap. 18:4. ‘And I heard another voice from Heaven, saying, come out of her my people that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues.’ Now if we have proved that mystery Babylon, the antitype of literal Babylon, which signifies confusion and mixture, represents the organized Churches of all descriptions, divided into three parts, Revelation 16:19, viz: Roman, Greek, and Protestant. Then is it not clear that this call from Heaven ‘come out of her my people,’ is a message delivered by messengers calling on God’s people to come out of these Churches” (BP2 65.3). He builds the OT-type chain Smith and Andrews leave implicit: “Isaiah says, ‘Depart ye, depart ye, go ye out from thence, — go ye out of the midst of her’... Jeremiah, speaking of literal Babylon, and John of mystical Babylon, shows clearly that the first is a type of the second... ‘Flee out of the midst of Babylon and deliver every man his soul’... Paul... ‘wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate’ (2 Cor 6:17, 18)” (BP2 67.2-68.2). Bates’ OT-type architecture (Isa 48; Jer 50-51; Zech 2; 2 Cor 6) is exactly the chain Bohr uses, three generations earlier.

Fitch’s 1843 sermon “Come Out of Her My People” is the original Millerite-era exposition of Rev 18:4 that fixed the verse’s ecclesiological identification of Babylon with apostate Protestantism. The surviving local-DB text is the March 1844 Midnight Cry letter where Fitch publicly enacts the message on himself: “our dear Brother Storrs says that the Great Head of the Church designed that we should come out of Babylon, and not wait for Babylon to thrust us out. I believe he is right, and I am therefore determined to come out” (LCFMC 1.1). He follows with his formal repudiation of his Congregational Association membership and creed-subscription: “I do from this time regard myself, and hereby proclaim myself to all men, as free and independent of all ecclesiastical domination, as a member of no sect, and a subscriber to no creed” (LCFMC 1.3). Fitch is therefore the practical pioneer of Rev 18:4 — the man whose 1843 sermon converted “come out of her” from a future-tense apocalyptic expectation into a present-tense ecclesiastical obligation. The entire later pioneer reading (Bates, J. White, Andrews, Smith) builds on Fitch’s identification.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 133-134
modernReadings.html: Babylon = papacy + apostate Protestant daughters; the call is typed by OT (Isa 48:20; Jer 50:8; 51:6, 45) — literal Israel out of literal Babylon to literal Jerusalem, spiritual Israel out of spiritual Babylon to spiritual Jerusalem (the SDA remnant). Verbatim: "Babylon is not only the papacy but also the apostate protestant churches" (p. 133).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Pioneer reading exactly. Rule XII (OT-NT trace) + Rule V (EGW's own exposition) + Rule IV (no contradiction with Rev 17 harlot/daughters framework).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.4)
notes.html: The OT-call-out trace (Isa 48:20; 52:11; Jer 50:8; 51:6, 45) and the literal-Israel/spiritual-Israel typological structure are standard pioneer typology but are not in Smith's v.4 paragraph. EGW _GC_ 390 is EGW corroboration, not DAR. Smith's emphasis falls on the _drama_ of the call (the suddenness, the sighing remnant of Ezek 9, the wash-of-hands separation), not on a Rule-XII OT-type chain.

verses.text: For her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads v.5 as the legal pivot triggering the call out: “Of Babylon, at the time brought to view in this prophecy, it is declared: ‘Her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities.’ Revelation 18:5. She has filled up the measure of her guilt, and destruction is about to fall upon her. But God still has a people in Babylon; and before the visitation of His judgments these faithful ones must be called out” (GC 604.1). The “remembered” clause functions for EGW as the judicial-juncture announcement — the cup of iniquity is full, the plagues are next, the only mercy left is the call to exit. This is the same forensic-remembrance reading Bohr unpacks lexically; EGW arrives at it via canonical typology (Gen 15:16 measure-of-iniquity, Sodom on the eve of destruction at EW 278.2) rather than Hebrew zakar word-study.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 672.1; specific comment within vv.4-8 block at DAR 672.1 on v.5
pioneerReadings.html: Smith dramatizes v.5 inside the v.4 setup: "Meanwhile, **great Babylon's sins mount up to the heavens, and the remembrance of her iniquities comes up before God. The storm of vengeance gathers. The great tidal wave of supernal wrath rolls onward.** The feathery foam plays along its crest, indicating that but an instant remains ere it will burst upon the great city of confusion, and proud Babylon will go down as a millstone sinks in the depths of the sea" (DAR 672.1). The cup-of-iniquity moment in Smith is rhetorical-eschatological — the divine wave is gathered and about to break — not unpacked through OT cup-filling parallels.

Andrews binds verse 5 to verse 4 as a single judicial unit: "Her sins have reached unto heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities; she has united herself to the kings of earth, and confided in the arm of flesh, and not in Jehovah. For the sake of this protection, she has smoothed down the terrible threatenings of God's word against sin, and has thrown the mantle of religion over some of the basest of human crimes" (TMR 63.2). The "sins reaching unto heaven" are, for Andrews, the cumulative ecclesiastical complicity in civil iniquity — and he cites the Fugitive Slave Law as a concrete contemporary instance. The verse therefore is not merely a poetic flourish before the lament begins; it is the indictment that justifies the call to come out (v.4) and the millstone-cast (v.21). The "remembering" is judicial, not psychological: God has now reached the bar at which the recorded sins demand judgment.

Haskell reads v.5 typologically against the ancient Babel pattern of Gen 11: “their iniquity reached unto heaven, and God came down to visit them. His coming brought confusion and consternation; and the languages of men were confounded so that they could not understand one another. Then the name Babel was applied, which means confusion” (SSP 301.1). The eschatological Babylon repeats the same arc: “the sins of the ancient city are repeated by the last church, and its overthrow is the object lesson, to the world, of the final destruction of the whole world when Christ comes down, because her iniquity has reached heaven. The figure is followed throughout the eighteenth chapter of Revelation” (SSP 301.2). Haskell explicitly chains v.5’s “sins reached unto heaven” to the call out: “For her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities. As the angels took Lot by the hands, and hurried him out of Sodom... so angels will hurry the sincere and true-hearted out of Babylon” (SSP 311.1).

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 134-135
modernReadings.html: "Remembered" = executive judgment, not awareness; God delays until the cup of iniquity is full (Gen 15:16; Rev 14:10; 16:19), then "remembers." Verbatim: "God's 'remembering' must be understood in the sense of God delaying the ultimate punishment until the cup of iniquity is full. That is to say, God delays punishment and when the cup is full, He 'remembers'" (p. 135).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule XII trace properly applied.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.5)
notes.html: The Gen 15:16 / Matt 23:32 / 1 Thess 2:16 / Rev 14:10; 16:19 trace and the Hebrew _zakar_ judicial-remembrance lexical observation (Gen 8:1; 19:29; Ex 2:24) are standard pioneer/Adventist exegesis but are not in Smith's v.5 commentary. Smith does not catalogue cup-of-iniquity texts; he treats v.5 as the rhetorical pivot point in the unfolding Loud-Cry drama.

verses.text: Reward her even as she rewarded you, and double unto her double according to her works: in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW does not exposit v.6 word-for-word, but her account of the eschatological lex-talionis matches Smith’s “reward her as she rewarded you” logic precisely: “the law will be invoked against commandment keepers. They will be threatened with fines and imprisonment... thus light will be brought before thousands who otherwise would know nothing of these truths” (GC 607.1) — followed by the corresponding plague-reward when probation closes: “many of the wicked were greatly enraged as they suffered the effects of the plagues. It was a scene of fearful agony” (EW 282.1). The double-recompense logic of v.6 is therefore for EGW the same logic that operates from the apostate-Protestant persecution of commandment-keepers (Smith’s “reward her as she rewarded you”) through the falling of the plagues. EGW does not engage the OT “double” chain (Isa 40:2; Jer 16:18) but anchors the same conclusion in the Sabbath-persecution narrative.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 673.2; 674.1; specific comment within vv.4-8 block at DAR 673.2 and 674.1 on v.6
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads v.6 as **judicial reciprocity directed especially at the apostate Protestant daughters**, when their newfound civil power produces a fresh wave of persecution against the saints: "Verses 6 and 7 are a prophetic declaration that she will be rewarded, or punished, according to her works... it must apply especially to the 'daughters,' the denominations who persist in clinging to the personal traits of the 'mother'... These, as pointed out on a previous page, are to attempt a **sweeping persecution against the truth and the people of God**. By these the 'image of the beast' is to be formed... The expression, 'Reward her even as she rewarded you,' seems to show that the time for this message to be given, and for the saints to be called out, will be when she begins to raise against them the arm of oppression. As she fills up the cup of persecution to the saints, so the angel of the Lord will persecute her (Psalms 35:6); and judgments from on high will bring upon her, in a twofold degree, the evil which she thought to bring upon the humble servants of the Lord" (DAR 673.2). Smith then cites EGW _Spiritual Gifts_ (= _Early Writings_ p. 137): "the first part of Revelation 18 has special reference to the religious oppression to be developed in the **United States by professed Christians**... 'It will be more tolerable for the heathen and for papists in the day of the execution of God's judgment than for such men... Reward her even as she rewarded you, double unto her double according to her works'" (DAR 674.1).

Haskell takes verse 6 as the principle of lex talionis applied to Babylon: "As the ancient city of Babylon was overthrown because she forsook the way of life, so modern Babylon dies" (SSP 313.1). The "rendering double" is not divine vindictiveness but the unfolding of the exact justice already inherent in Babylon's history. What she did to the saints — silencing them in the dust for forty-two months (Rev 11:2-3; SSP 312.2), drinking the blood of prophets (Rev 18:24) — is now poured back into her cup. Haskell ties this to the long-deferred plea of the fifth seal: "How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth?" (Rev 6:10). The doubling of verse 6 is the divine answer to that question.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 137
modernReadings.html: No discrete verse-6 exegesis; covered under the vv.6-8 "Babylon's Reward" block, where Bohr lets the text and an EGW citation (TM 62) carry the lex-talionis logic. Verbatim setup: "Render to her just as she rendered to you, and repay her double according to her works; in the cup which she has mixed, mix double for her" (p. 137, quoting the verse).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.6)
notes.html: The Isa 40:2 / Jer 16:18; 17:18 / Zech 9:12 "double" trace is standard pioneer typology but is not in Smith's v.6 paragraph; Smith anchors only to Ps 35:6 ("the angel of the Lord will persecute her"). The lex-talionis label and the "kings of Rev 17:16-17 as executioners" reading are extra structural framing — Smith identifies the executioner not as the kings of Rev 17 but as "the angel of the Lord" / divine judgment.

verses.text: How much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously, so much torment and sorrow give her: for she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow.

pioneerReadings.html: White quotes verse 7 verbatim within her composite Rev 18 quotation: "How much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously, so much torment and sorrow give her: for she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow" (GC 653.1). The self-flattery of "I sit a queen" is, on White's reading, the prophetic indictment of the apostate church's political pretensions — the union of religious power with civil power that constitutes Babylon's essence (cf. GC 588). She is "no widow" because she is married to the kings of the earth (Rev 17:2); she "shall see no sorrow" because she has convinced herself that the alliance is permanent. The verse therefore is not a passing taunt; it is the precise charge that justifies the plagues of verse 8: Babylon's self-confidence in her ecclesiastical-civil alliance is the very arrogance that makes her ripe for destruction.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 673.2; specific comment within vv.4-8 block at DAR 673.2 on v.7
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads "I sit a queen, and am no widow" as **the boast of the apostate Protestant daughters intoxicated by their newly acquired civil power**, not as the papacy's _Ecclesia perpetua_ boast: "it is doubtless this first intoxication of power that leads this branch of Babylon to cherish in her heart the boast, 'I sit a queen, and am no widow;' that is, **I am no longer χήρα, 'one bereaved,' or destitute of power, as I have been; but now I rule like a queen; I shall see no sorrow; God is in the Constitution; the church is enthroned, and shall henceforth bear sway**" (DAR 673.2). Smith provides the Greek (_chēra_ = "one bereaved") and reads the boast as the moment when Protestantism in the United States, freshly empowered to "form the image" and use the civil arm to enforce its dogmas, exults in its end of bereavement.

Jones reads “I sit a queen” structurally as the inevitable boast of a re-empowered papacy whose authority is restored by the apostate-Protestant making of the image to the beast: “the apostasy of Protestantism exalts the papacy, because this is an open confession to the world by professed Protestantism that the papal principles alone are correct. The making of the Image to the Beast restores and magnifies the power of the Beast — Revelation 13:12... once more she guides and dominates the nations. Consequently she glorifies herself and lives deliciously; the kings of the earth commit fornication and live deliciously with her, as did the false prophets with Jezebel of old; and therefore she congratulates herself, saying in her heart, ‘I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow.’ And saith the Lord: ‘Therefore shall her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine; and she shall be utterly burned with fire; for strong is the Lord God who judgeth her’” (ECE 873.1). Jones’ reading is closer to Bohr’s (papal self-exaltation) than to Smith’s US-Protestant reading; he reads the boast as a consequence of restored papal supremacy enabled by Protestant apostasy.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 137-138
modernReadings.html: The queen/widow boast is typed directly from Isa 47:8-9; Babylon will be widowed when both her lovers (the kings) and her children (the Protestant denominations) turn on her. Verbatim: "Babylon will be a widow because she will lose the support of her lovers, the kings, and she will lose the support of her children, the Protestant denominations… She will come to her end with none to help her (Daniel 11:45)" (p. 138).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule XII (Isa 47 → Rev 18 type/antitype trace). Rule IV correctly cross-links Rev 17:16-17 (kings turn on harlot) and Dan 11:45 (none to help) — but note: Bohr's Dan 11:45 cross-link depends on his spiritualized Dan 11:45 reading, which itself fails Rule XI (see Dan 11 section above).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.7)
notes.html: The Isa 47:7-8 type/antitype trace is standard pioneer typology but is not in Smith's v.7 paragraph; Smith makes no reference to literal Babylon's pre-Cyrus boast. The _Ecclesia perpetua_ + Lateran inscription material is not in DAR at all. Smith's reading is **distinctively US-Protestant** ("God is in the Constitution"), not Roman.

verses.text: Therefore shall her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine; and she shall be utterly burned with fire: for strong is the Lord God who judgeth her.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW identifies the plagues of v.8 with the seven last plagues of Rev 15-16, falling on the apostate-church confederacy that has rejected the third angel’s message: “[Revelation 18:1-8 quoted.] This terrible picture, drawn by John to show how completely the powers of earth will give themselves over to evil, should show those who have received the truth how dangerous it is to link up with secret societies or to join themselves in any way with those who do not keep God’s commandments” (7BC 985.7). On the “one day” timeframe and the suddenness: “the wicked have passed the boundary of their probation; the Spirit of God, persistently resisted, has been at last withdrawn... Satan will then plunge the inhabitants of the earth into one great, final trouble” (GC 614.1). And the apostate-church confederacy specifically: “the forms of religion will be continued by a people from whom the Spirit of God has been finally withdrawn; and the satanic zeal with which the prince of evil will inspire them for the accomplishment of his malignant designs, will bear the semblance of zeal for God” (GC 615.1). EGW does not engage Smith’s day-for-a-year question on “one day,” but reads the plague-fall as proximate, irreversible, and concentrated.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 674.2; specific comment within vv.4-8 block at DAR 674.2 on v.8
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads "in one day" as a **prophetic day**: "The day in which her plagues come, mentioned in verse 8, must be a prophetic day, or at least cannot be a literal day; for it would be impossible for famine to come in that length of time. The plagues of Babylon are without doubt the seven last plagues, which have already been examined; and the plain inference from the language of this verse, in connection with **Isaiah 34:8**, is that **a year will be occupied in that terrible visitation**" (DAR 674.2). So "one day" = roughly one year of plague-execution (day-for-a-year applied to Isa 34:8's "day of the Lord's vengeance").

Andrews ties v.8’s plagues directly to the seventh vial of Rev 16:17-21: “the destruction of Babylon, as described in Revelation 18, takes place under the seventh vial; for it is under that vial that she comes in remembrance before God, to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath. Revelation 16:17-21” (TMR 62.2). He cross-links to Rev 19 to fix the chronology: “it is manifest that the destruction of Babylon begins before the great battle takes place; for the kings are spared to witness her destruction (Revelation 18); but in the great battle they are all destroyed. Revelation 19. While the saints eat the marriage supper, they behold the smoke of her burning; and in response to the call of Revelation 18:20, they rejoice over her. Revelation 19:1-9” (TMR 62.2). Andrews’ framework therefore: the “one day” plague-fall is the seventh-vial event, not a prophetic-day duration; the kings survive long enough to lament (vv.9-10) before being destroyed at Armageddon (Rev 19). This is a different chronological reading from Smith’s “one day = year of recompense” (Isa 34:8) reading.

Haskell ties “utterly burned with fire” to the seventh plague’s climactic earthquake-and-conflagration scene: “the seventh angel pours out his vial in the air. The elements... clash together; and above the tumult, the mighty peals of thunder, and the flashes of lightning, the voice of Jehovah Himself is heard saying, ‘It is done.’ ‘All the host of heaven shall be dissolved... For my sword shall be bathed in heaven... for it is the day of the Lord’s vengeance, and the year of recompences for the controversy of Zion’” (SSP 285.1). The Isa 34:8 anchor (“year of recompences”) ties Haskell back to Smith’s day-for-a-year reading. The fire of v.8 is therefore for Haskell the combined plague-execution and final conflagration that consumes Babylon at the seventh vial.

Bates reads verse 8 as the logical consequence of the second angel's message once it has been rejected: "It was now obvious that she had drank from the cup of poison so deeply, that it had seized her vitals. She therefore utterly rejected her coming Lord" (BP2 64.2). The plagues of verse 8 — death, mourning, famine, burning — fall in one day because Babylon's rejection of the loud cry has terminated her probation. Bates's order is sequential: the first angel's message proclaims the hour of judgment; the second angel's message proclaims her fall in answer to her rejection of the first; the third angel's warning of the wine of God's wrath (Rev 14:10) is then poured out as the plagues of verse 8. The "strong" Lord who judges her is, for Bates, the same Mighty Angel of Rev 10 and Rev 18:1 who came down to enlighten the earth — His strength is now revealed in judgment because His mercy was refused.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 137
modernReadings.html: No discrete verse-8 exegesis; covered under the vv.6-8 "Babylon's Reward" block via an EGW citation (TM 62) pointing the plagues at the apostate-church confederacy that exalts Satan. Verbatim from the cited EGW: "to the apostate churches that unite in the exaltation of Satan, the sentence will go forth, 'Therefore shall her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine; and she shall be utterly burned with fire'" (p. 137, quoting TM 62). Bohr does not engage the day-for-a-year question.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.8)
notes.html: The Isa 47:9 cross-link, the Ezek 14:21 fourfold-judgment trace, and the Lev 21:9 "burned-with-fire = priest's-daughter penalty" type are not in Smith's v.8 paragraph. Smith's only OT anchor for v.8 is **Isa 34:8** (the year-of-recompense for Zion's controversy). The "strong is the Lord God" rhetorical inversion of v.7's "I sit a queen" is interpretive synthesis, not in Smith.

verses.text: And the kings of the earth, who have committed fornication and lived deliciously with her, shall bewail her, and lament for her, when they shall see the smoke of her burning.

pioneerReadings.html: Ellen White quotes verses 7-10 directly: "For she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow. Therefore shall her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine; and she shall be utterly burned with fire: for strong is the Lord God who judgeth her. And the kings of the earth, who have committed fornication and lived deliciously with her, shall bewail her, and lament for her..." (GC 653.1, quoting Rev 18:7-9). For White, the lament of the kings is not sympathy but self-interested terror; the political powers that supported Babylon now see their own ecclesiastical-civil order disintegrating. They stand "afar off" (v.10) because they fear partaking of her torment, exactly as the merchants will in verses 11-17 and the shipmasters in verses 17-19. The lament is the inversion of the loud cry: those who would not heed the call to come out now mourn at a distance.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 675.2; specific comment within vv.9-11 block at DAR 675.2 on v.9
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads vv.9-11 as one unit titled "A Fitting Retribution" — and his framing is **post-plague economic collapse**, not Rev-17-kings-turning-on-the-harlot: "The infliction of the very first plague must result in a complete suspension of traffic in those articles of luxury for which Babylon is noted. And when the merchants of these things, who are to a great extent citizens of this symbolic city, and who have been made rich by their traffic in these things, suddenly find themselves and their neighbors smitten with putrefying sores, their traffic suspended, and vast stores of merchandise on hand, but none to buy them, **they lift up their voices in lamentation for the fate of this great city; for if there is anything which will draw from the men of this generation a sincere cry of distress, it is that which touches their treasures**" (DAR 675.2).

Haskell reads the kings’ lament in vv.9-10 typologically against the literal Babylon’s prophesied desolation (Isa 13-14): “the prophecies concerning her downfall depicted complete ruin, and travellers to-day corroborate the words of Isaiah, ‘Babylon, the glory of kingdoms, the beauty of the Chaldees’ excellency, shall be as when God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah’” (SSP 303.1). The economic-power emphasis comes through in his US application of Babylon’s lament: “in the United States, the formation of the image to the beast, will place the unbounded resources of this country in the hands of the same power... the distinction between the rich and the poor, becomes more marked; the corporations and trusts control the money, the produce, and the laboring classes. Democracy gives way to a king, — the coal king, the oil king, or the money king. A few men dictate to the masses” (SSP 308.1). Haskell anticipates Bohr’s “capitalism” reading by over a century — the kings who lament are the modern industrial-financial monopolists whose civic system collapses when Babylon falls.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 138
modernReadings.html: "The Lament of the Kings" (heading); Bohr treats vv.9-10 as a single chorus of kings who fornicated with the harlot now mourning her burning. He provides no extended v.9 exegesis beyond the heading and the quoted text; substantive comment lands on v.10. Verbatim: "These verses describe… The kings of the earth who committed fornication and lived luxuriously with her will weep and lament for her" (p. 138, quoting the verse under his "Lament of the Kings" header).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.9)
notes.html: The structural label "three lamentation choruses" (kings / merchants / mariners) is standard pioneer architectural framing but is not in Smith's v.9 paragraph; Smith does not separately introduce a kings-chorus. The Gen 19:28 / Isa 34:10 / Rev 14:11; 19:3 "smoke" trace is not in Smith's v.9 paragraph. The Rev 17:13 / 17:16-17 cross-link (kings give power then turn) is interpretive overlay; Smith reads the kings simply as those caught in the first-plague-triggered economic collapse.

verses.text: Standing afar off for the fear of her torment, saying, Alas, alas, that great city Babylon, that mighty city! for in one hour is thy judgment come.

pioneerReadings.html: "Alas, alas that great city Babylon, that mighty city! for in one hour is thy judgment come" — Ellen White quotes verse 10 directly in her treatment of Babylon's end (GC 653.1). She does not allegorize the kings as anything other than the civil rulers who have allied themselves with the apostate religious system; their cry shows they have lost the very thing they expected ecclesiastical alliance to secure. The "one hour" is for White a prophetic emphasis on suddenness, not a literal twenty-four-period; it matches the "one day" of verse 8 and the millstone hurled into the sea of verse 21 — all three figures stress that the collapse, when it comes, comes faster than the principals can comprehend. The kings stand "afar off, for fear of her torment," a phrase White returns to when describing post-probation despair (cf. GC 654).

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 675.2-676.0; specific comment within vv.9-11 block at DAR 675.3 on v.10
pioneerReadings.html: Smith provides a careful gloss on "standing afar off": it is **figurative, not literal flight** — "The question may arise how persons involved in the same calamity can stand afar off and lament, etc.; but it must be remembered that this desolation is brought to view under a figure, and the figure is that of a city visited with destruction. Should calamity come upon a literal city, it would be natural for its inhabitants to flee from that city if they had opportunity, and standing afar off, lament its fall; and just in proportion to their terror and amazement at the evil impending, would be the distance at which they would stand from their devoted city. Now the figure the apostle uses would not be complete without a feature of this kind; and so he uses it, not to imply that people would literally flee from the symbolic city, which would be impossible, but to denote their **terror and amazement** at the descending judgments" (DAR 675.3). On the buy-sell reversal: "there is a fitness in this retribution. **They who but a short time before had issued a decree that the saints of God should neither buy nor sell, now find themselves put under the same restriction by a far more effectual process**" (DAR 675.2 close).

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 138
modernReadings.html: Greek "judgment" here = _krísis_ (execution of the sentence), not _kríma_ (the sentence itself, as in Rev 17:1) — chapter 17 = sentence, chapter 18 = execution; "standing afar off" = moral distancing, not geographic flight. Verbatim: "In other words, the fate of the kings is inexorably bound up with hers. They did not listen to the call to come out of her and therefore they shared in her sins and received her plagues" (p. 138).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Greek lexical work + Rule IV (Rev 17 / Rev 18 cross-link).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.10)
notes.html: The Ezek 26:17-18 / 27:30-32 Tyre-elegiac type/antitype trace is not in Smith's v.10 paragraph; Smith does not anchor the "Alas, alas" formula in Tyre. The 1260-year-buildup vs "one hour" compression contrast is structural overlay; Smith reads "one hour" as the timeframe of the lamentation-as-figure, not as a chronological compression statement.

verses.text: And the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her; for no man buyeth their merchandise any more.

pioneerReadings.html: White quotes verse 11 directly as part of her quotation of the lament: "The merchants of the earth, that have waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies, shall stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing" (GC 653.2, quoting Rev 18:11, 3, 15-17). She reads the merchants' weeping as commercial loss — the entire economy built on the union of false religion and civil power suddenly worthless. The "delicacies" that enriched them are, in White's framework, the doctrines and indulgences of the apostate system that compromised the gospel for political and financial gain. GC 654.1 expands this: "the rich prided themselves upon their superiority to those who were less favored; but they had obtained their riches by violation of the law of God. They had neglected to feed the hungry, to clothe the naked, to deal justly, and to love mercy." The merchants' lament is therefore the just exposure of the social-economic injustice intrinsic to Babylon's system.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 675.2; specific comment within vv.9-11 block at DAR 675.2 on v.11
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads v.11 inside the same "Fitting Retribution" framing — the merchants are **the very citizens of Babylon** caught in the first-plague aftermath: "the merchants of these things, who are to a great extent **citizens of this symbolic city**, and who have been made rich by their traffic in these things, suddenly find themselves and their neighbors smitten with putrefying sores, their traffic suspended, and vast stores of merchandise on hand, but none to buy them" (DAR 675.2). Their grief is exposed as mercenary — "if there is anything which will draw from the men of this generation a sincere cry of distress, it is that which touches their treasures" (DAR 675.2).

Haskell’s reading of vv.11-13 quotes the entire merchandise catalogue verbatim and reads it as the antitypical fulfillment of literal Babylon’s Isa 13/47 desolation: “this was literally fulfilled in the earthly kingdom, Babylon; and it is placed on record in the inspired Word, that men may there read the result of such principles as were brought into practice in Babylon the Great” (SSP 304.1). The merchants who weep are read against Haskell’s US-monopolist application: “the corporations and trusts control the money, the produce, and the laboring classes” (SSP 308.1). For Haskell, when the eschatological Babylon collapses, the trust-and-monopoly economic system she built collapses with her — the same socio-economic indictment Bohr modernizes into “Amazons, Facebooks, Googles.”

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 139
modernReadings.html: "The Lament of the Capitalists" (heading); the wailing merchants = the "great men of the earth" (18:23), modernized as today's tech-capitalist mega-corporations. Verbatim: "In that day the Amazons, the Facebooks, and the Googles of the world will see that the accumulation of wealth at the expense of the poor has cost them eternal life" (p. 139).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **No violation, but interpretive expansion.** The "capitalism" label is Bohr's modernizing rhetoric, not a symbol redefinition. He correctly applies Rule XII (Ezek 27's Tyre-merchant trace as the background for Rev 18's merchant lament). _This is Bohr's most original contribution to Adventist Revelation exposition and it does not break Miller's Rules._

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.11)
notes.html: The Ezek 27 Tyre-commerce-lament trace as background-type for Rev 18 is standard pioneer / commentator typology but is not invoked in Smith's v.11 paragraph. The "moral character of the commercial system / sustained by the demand of a corrupted civilization" socio-economic gloss is interpretive overlay; Smith's framing is narrowly that the first plague's putrefying sores collapse the trade in luxury goods that fueled Babylon's culture.

verses.text: The merchandise of gold, and silver, and precious stones, and of pearls, and fine linen, and purple, and silk, and scarlet, and all thyine wood, and all manner vessels of ivory, and all manner vessels of most precious wood, and of brass, and iron, and marble.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 676.2; specific comment within vv.12-13 block at DAR 676.2 on v.12
pioneerReadings.html: Smith gives the v.12 inventory only a single broad summary line under the heading "Babylon's Merchandise" — and his reading is **moralistic-thematic**, not item-by-item exegesis: "In these verses we have an enumeration of great Babylon's merchandise, which includes **everything pertaining to luxurious living, pomp, and worldly display**. All kinds of mercantile traffic are brought to view" (DAR 676.2).

Jones reads the merchandise catalogue (especially the v.12-14 luxury-trade items) within the historicist framework of the “ten persecutions” theme — though his explicit verse-reference is to the “ten horns” that turn on the harlot, not the merchandise list itself: “in the church and State scheme of the fourth century, the theory of the bishops was that the kingdom of God was come; and to maintain the theory it became necessary to pervert the meaning of both Scripture history and Scripture prophecy. Accordingly, as the antitype of the ten plagues of Egypt, and as the fulfilment of the prophecy of the ten horns which made war with the Lamb (Revelation 18:12-14), there was invented the theory of ten persecutions of the Christians inflicted by the ten emperors” (GEP 372.3). Note: Jones cites Rev 18:12-14 in connection with the “ten horns” (a probable misreference for Rev 17:12-14 or Rev 18:9 kings), but his use of the verse-range as a Catholic-church-historiography polemic is a distinctively pioneer angle — the merchandise items represent the luxury-and-power apparatus that the apostate church traded for civil empire.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 141
modernReadings.html: The 28-item inventory mixes staples and luxuries to show Babylon's total stranglehold on world commerce; modernized for today, the list would be "stocks, bonds, I-Phones, I-Pads… fancy automobiles and mansions." Verbatim: "the main point is that before her fall, Babylon had a stranglehold on all the commerce of the world and used it to oppress God's people" (p. 141).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.12)
notes.html: The Ezek 27:12-25 Tyre-catalogue parallel and the structural metals → jewels → fabrics → wood-ivory-stone parsing are standard pioneer / commentator typology but are not unpacked in Smith's v.12 paragraph. The modernizing application (silicon / lithium / rare earths) is a contemporary extension; Smith does not modernize the list. Smith's v.12 commentary is one sentence indexing the whole inventory as "luxurious living, pomp, and worldly display."

verses.text: And cinnamon, and odours, and ointments, and frankincense, and wine, and oil, and fine flour, and wheat, and beasts, and sheep, and horses, and chariots, and slaves, and souls of men.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 676.2; specific comment within vv.12-13 block at DAR 676.2 on v.13
pioneerReadings.html: **SUBSTANTIVE CORRECTION**: Smith does **not** read "slaves and souls of men" as literal slave-trade. He explicitly spiritualizes the pair: "The declaration concerning '**slaves and souls of men**' may pertain more particularly to **the spiritual domain**, and have reference to **slavery of conscience by the creeds of these bodies, which in some cases is more oppressive than physical bondage**" (DAR 676.2). The whole v.12-13 inventory functions for Smith as "everything pertaining to luxurious living, pomp, and worldly display" (DAR 676.2 head), and "slaves and souls of men" is read as the apostate denominations' creedal coercion of conscience.

Loughborough preserves the most famous historical use of Rev 18:13 in early Adventist literature — the “binding of the souls of men” clause functioning as a literal-creedal indictment of priestly authority. He recounts an evangelistic encounter in which a boy challenges a priest: “‘Will you please read for me Revelation 18:13?’ ‘Yes,’ said the priest. As he read the people counted, and sure enough the word and was there just fourteen times, and among the fourteen times was the ‘binding of the souls of men.’ The people shouted, ‘There! there! the boy knows more about the Bible than the priest!’” (GSAM 145.1). Loughborough does not give an exegetical reading of the “slaves and souls of men” clause, but the rhetorical force is consistent with the pioneer spiritualization Smith makes at DAR 676.2 (creedal slavery of conscience under fallen-church authority).

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 141
modernReadings.html: Reads the pair "bodies and souls of men" through Matt 10:28 — "bodies" = present physical life, "souls" = spiritual/eternal destiny; Babylon traded with both. Verbatim: "Babylon not only traded with the physical bodies of human beings but also toyed with their very salvation and eternal destiny" (p. 141).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **No clear violation against the DAR pioneer reading.** Smith himself spiritualizes "slaves" as slavery-of-conscience under fallen-church creeds, treating the pair "slaves and souls of men" as pertaining to "the spiritual domain" (DAR 676.2). Bohr's body/soul ontology reading (Matt 10:28 physical/spiritual life) reaches for a slightly different spiritualization but stays inside the same hermeneutic register Smith opened. A _literalist_ Rule-XI critique (Greek _sōma_ in commercial papyri / Ezek 27:13 LXX _sōmasin anthrōpōn_ → Roman / medieval / transatlantic slave-trade) is an exegetically defensible alternative, but it is **not the DAR pioneer reading**; the prior version of this row mis-attributed that literalist reading to Smith. On the DAR pioneer baseline, Bohr is rule-compliant here.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: None (Smith and Bohr both spiritualize; the differences are intra-spiritual lexical choices, not symbol redefinition).

verses.text: And the fruits that thy soul lusted after are departed from thee, and all things which were dainty and goodly are departed from thee, and thou shalt find them no more at all.

pioneerReadings.html: Smith titles v.14 "Gluttony Rebuked" and reads it concretely: "The fruits here mentioned are, according to the original, **'autumnal fruits;'** and in this we find a prophecy that **the 'delicacies of the season,' upon which the luxurious gormand so sets his pampered appetite, will be suddenly cut off**. This, of course, is the work of the famine, which is the result of the fourth vial. Chapter 16:8. And we may be even now having a premonition of this destruction in the **phylloxera of the vineyards, the 'yellows' of the peach orchards, and other recent enemies to vegetation**" (DAR 676.4). For Smith the verse is (a) lexical (the Greek points to autumnal fruits / seasonal delicacies), (b) thematically an indictment of luxurious gluttony, (c) chronologically tied to the fourth-plague famine (Rev 16:8), and (d) typologically prefigured in 19th-century crop-blight phenomena.

Haskell folds verse 14 into his picture of Babylon's end-state in SSP 312.2: "the social gatherings and the marriage feasts no longer offer any attraction"; the fruits she lusted after are gone because the very faculty for enjoyment is broken. He treats the verse as the inner counterpart of the outward lament that follows: before the kings, merchants, and shipmasters wail (vv.10, 16, 19), Babylon herself has already lost the appetite she lived for. The "dainty and goodly" things — Haskell's reading parallels his treatment of the worldly luxury of the apostate Protestant churches in SSP 308.2 (the high-priced pews, the paid singers, the popular ministers whose sermons "please the ear, but do not convert the heart"). The fruits of verse 14 are the very entertainments by which Babylon distinguished herself; their disappearance is the necessary precondition to the lament of verses 15-19.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html:
No violation.
**Bohr's reading (_Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 139-141):** No discrete verse-14 exegesis; covered under the "Lament of the Capitalists" block (vv. 11-16) where the wicked lament not because they have sinned but because they have lost their riches. Verbatim: "The wicked do not lament because they have sinned. They lament because they have lost their riches and with their riches, eternal life" (p. 139).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.14)
notes.html: The "direct address to Babylon as parenthetical apostrophe interrupting third-person lament" rhetorical-structure observation is not in Smith's v.14 paragraph. The "threefold 'no more at all'" structural-marker analysis spanning vv.14, 21, 22, 23 is interpretive overlay; Smith does not catalogue the formula across the chapter. Smith reads v.14 about famine-cut-off of gluttonous consumption, not about the eschatological permanence of Babylon's disappearance.

verses.text: The merchants of these things, which were made rich by her, shall stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing.

pioneerReadings.html: White's GC 653 paragraph on the lament explicitly includes verses 15-17 in her composite quotation: the merchants who "made her rich" now "stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing." Verse 15 marks the moment the commercial-religious alliance dissolves under the weight of God's wrath; the merchants who once flourished by Babylon's patronage now have no city to trade with. White's exposition of GC 654.1 amplifies the indictment behind this lament: the rich gained their wealth by violating God's law, neglecting the poor, and seeking the homage of their fellow creatures — and now they are "stripped of all that made them great." For White, verse 15 is the prophetic vindication of every neglected justice claim that Babylon ignored during her prosperity.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 678.2; specific comment within vv.15-19 block at DAR 678.2 on v.15
