pioneerReadings.html: Smith treats vv.15-19 as one unit titled "Emotions of the Wicked" and reads the merchants' lament concretely against the seven plagues already poured: "Imagine **the plague of sores preying upon men, the rivers turned to blood, the sea like the blood of a dead man, the sun scorching men with fire, their traffic gone, and their silver and gold unable to deliver them**, and we need not wonder at their exclamations of distress, nor that shipmasters and sailors join in the general wail" (DAR 678.2). The afar-off-fear posture is the natural response to the unfolding plague-scenes of Rev 16, not a structural-rhetorical marker that Smith calls out.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html:
No violation.
**Bohr's reading (_Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 139-140):** No discrete verse-15 exegesis; covered under the "Lament of the Capitalists" block (vv. 11-16), where the merchants' "weeping and wailing" is unpacked through four intense Greek words (klaío, penthéo, kópto, krázo). Verbatim: "The extreme anguish of the wicked is expressed with several intense Greek words" (p. 140).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.15)
notes.html: The "second 'standing afar off'" cataloguing across vv.10, 15, 17 as a structural-pattern marker, and the three-tier political/commercial/logistical taxonomy, are interpretive overlays not in Smith's v.15 paragraph. Smith reads v.15 as part of one continuous wicked-emotional response to the plagues.

verses.text: And saying, Alas, alas, that great city, that was clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet, and decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls!

pioneerReadings.html: White quotes verse 16 verbatim in her composite Rev 18 quotation: "Alas, alas that great city, that was clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet, and decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls!" (GC 653.2, quoting Rev 18:16). The wardrobe matches exactly the description of the harlot of Rev 17:4, confirming that Babylon of chapter 18 and the woman on the beast of chapter 17 are the same prophetic figure. The fine linen, purple, scarlet, and gold stand for the borrowed righteousness, royal pretension, blood-guilt, and material wealth of the apostate religious system. White's pairing of this verse with GC 654 makes the moral inversion explicit: the very ornaments by which Babylon distinguished herself become the badges of her shame on the day she is stripped.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 678.2; specific comment within vv.15-19 block at DAR 678.2 on v.16
pioneerReadings.html: Smith has no dedicated v.16 commentary; the verse falls inside the "Emotions of the Wicked" unit (DAR 678.2). The "Alas, alas" lament is read as part of the universal voice of mourning from those whose "traffic [is] gone, and their silver and gold unable to deliver them" (DAR 678.2). Smith does not unpack the finery catalogue separately.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html:
No violation.
**Bohr's reading (_Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 139-141):** No discrete verse-16 exegesis; covered under the "Lament of the Capitalists" block (vv. 11-16) — the merchants who weep are "the great men of the earth" (18:23), the contemporary tech-finance elites. Verbatim: "In that day the Amazons, the Facebooks, and the Googles of the world will see that the accumulation of wealth at the expense of the poor has cost them eternal life" (p. 139).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.16)
notes.html: The cross-link to Rev 17:4 (identical harlot's robe → harlot and city are the same entity) is a sound Rule-IV move but is not made in Smith's v.16 paragraph. The Ezek 26:17-18 / 27:30-32 Tyre-elegiac trace is standard pioneer typology but is not invoked here. Smith reads v.16 as one beat in the continuous wicked-emotional response, not as a structural pivot.

verses.text: For in one hour so great riches is come to nought. And every shipmaster, and all the company in ships, and sailors, and as many as trade by sea, stood afar off.

pioneerReadings.html: White concludes the merchants' lament with verse 17: "For in one hour so great riches is come to nought" (GC 653.2). The "one hour" echoes the "one hour" of verse 10 and the "one day" of verse 8 — for White, all three are prophetic notations of suddenness, not literal time spans. The shipmasters and sailors of verse 17b enter as a third class of mourners after the kings (vv.9-10) and merchants (vv.11-16); each represents a constituency that profited by Babylon's alliance with civil power. The lament has a tripled structure because Babylon's power was tripled: political (the kings), economic (the merchants), and commercial-maritime (the shipmasters). When the millstone falls (v.21), all three lose their source of wealth at once.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 678.2; specific comment within vv.15-19 block at DAR 678.2 on v.17
pioneerReadings.html: Smith addresses v.17 only inside the "Emotions of the Wicked" general comment: "their traffic gone, and their silver and gold unable to deliver them... we need not wonder at their exclamations of distress, nor that **shipmasters and sailors join in the general wail**" (DAR 678.2). The mariners are simply added to the merchants in one continuous wicked-mourning chorus; Smith does not separately analyze the seafaring tier.

Andrews uses verses 17-19 to refute the literalist reading of Babylon as a rebuilt Mesopotamian city. "If Babylon is a literal city, it must be a place of the greatest commercial importance; for in Revelation 18 it is represented as the great center of commerce, and its destruction causes universal mourning among the merchants and sailors of the world. Of course, if Babylon is yet to be a literal city, it must surpass in commercial importance every other city in the world; for the cessation of its trade must produce mourning among the merchants and sailors of every land" (TMR 42.3). Andrews points out that no candidate literal Babylon could satisfy this; only a symbolic Babylon identified with the world's apostate religious-commercial system fits. The shipmasters of verse 17 and the sailors of verse 19 are, on his reading, the actual nineteenth-century commercial powers of New York and London whose fortunes were intertwined with the religious establishment.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 141
modernReadings.html: Labels vv. 17-19 "The Lament of the Employees and Travelers" — the working-class tier (shipmasters, sailors, traders) distinct from the capitalist merchants of vv. 11-16; minimal exegesis beyond the structural label. Verbatim: "Verses 17-19: The Lament of the Employees and Travelers" (p. 141).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation, though the "employees and travelers" label is interpretive coloring rather than direct exegesis.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.17)
notes.html: The "in one hour" thrice-repetition cataloguing across vv.10, 17, 19 + the "compression-of-judgment vs centuries-of-buildup" reading is not in Smith's v.17 paragraph. The Ezek 27:25-36 ships-of-Tarshish parallel is standard pioneer typology but is not invoked here. The three-lamentation-chorus structural label is not Smith's framing — he reads vv.15-19 as one wicked-emotional unit.

verses.text: And cried when they saw the smoke of her burning, saying, What city is like unto this great city!

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 678.2; specific comment within vv.15-19 block at DAR 678.2 on v.18
pioneerReadings.html: Smith offers no dedicated v.18 commentary; the "What city is like unto this great city" exclamation falls inside the "Emotions of the Wicked" unit (DAR 678.2), where the universal voice of mourning is read as the natural reaction to the seven-plague onslaught.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html:
No violation.
**Bohr's reading (_Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 140-141):** No discrete verse-18 exegesis; covered under the "Lament of the Employees and Travelers" block (vv. 17-19), where the verb krázo ("cry") appears twice and links the wicked's cry to Jesus' cry on the cross and the cry of withheld wages. Verbatim: "The fourth word is krázo that is translated 'cry'. This appears word appears twice in Revelation 18:18, 19" (p. 140).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.18)
notes.html: The "incomparability of the city" reading, the contrast between the saints' knowledge of her uniqueness vs the wicked's mercenary uniqueness-acclamation, and the parsing of "What city is like unto this" as confession-via-lament are all interpretive overlays not in Smith's v.18 paragraph.

verses.text: And they cast dust on their heads, and cried, weeping and wailing, saying, Alas, alas, that great city, wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her costliness! for in one hour is she made desolate.

pioneerReadings.html: White's composite quotation of the lament includes verse 19's shipmaster figure indirectly through her quotation of verses 9-17. The "alas, alas, that great city" of verse 19 is the third repetition of the same threnody (with verses 10 and 16) and confirms the structural design of the lament. For White, the three "alas, alas" cries from kings, merchants, and shipmasters are the prophetic answer to the threefold loud cry of the first, second, and third angels (cf. EW 277-278): every voice that refused to come out at the loud cry now finds itself outside the city only because it is collapsing. The "dust on their heads" of verse 19 is the historic Eastern sign of utter, irretrievable loss; the shipmasters mourn not the city but the trade that made them.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 678.2; specific comment within vv.15-19 block at DAR 678.2 on v.19
pioneerReadings.html: Smith offers no dedicated v.19 commentary; the dust-on-heads + "Alas, alas" + "in one hour" all fall inside the single "Emotions of the Wicked" gloss (DAR 678.2) — the universal voice of mourning of those whose silver and gold cannot deliver them under the plagues.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html:
No violation.
**Bohr's reading (_Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 140):** Reads the dust-on-heads gesture against the OT mourning-gesture chain (Josh 7:6; Job 2:12; Lam 2:10; Ezek 27:30). Verbatim: "Another sign of the affliction and angst of the wicked is the sprinkling of dust upon their heads" (p. 140).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.19)
notes.html: The OT-mourning-gesture trace (Josh 7:6; 1 Sam 4:12; 2 Sam 1:2; 13:19; Job 2:12; Lam 2:10; Ezek 27:30) is standard pioneer typology but is not invoked in Smith's v.19 paragraph. The "third 'Alas, alas' + third 'in one hour' = completed lamentation pattern" structural reading and the three-choruses architecture (kings/merchants/mariners) are interpretive overlays not in Smith's framing.

verses.text: Rejoice over her, thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets; for God hath avenged you on her.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW does not exposit v.20 directly but her account of the saints’ vindication and the wicked’s self-reproach captures the same antiphonal logic Bohr identifies (earthly lament inverts to heavenly rejoicing). At the falling of the plagues: “parents were bitterly reproaching their children, and children their parents, brothers their sisters, and sisters their brothers. Loud, wailing cries were heard in every direction, ‘It was you who kept me from receiving the truth which would have saved me from this awful hour.’ The people turned upon their ministers with bitter hate and reproached them” (EW 282.1). The corresponding heaven-side rejoicing is in EGW’s parallel description of the saints’ deliverance and Christ’s coronation (EW 281.1: “Then I saw Jesus lay off His priestly attire and clothe Himself with His most kingly robes”) — the structural inversion is exactly what Bohr names, though EGW reaches it through narrative not Greek lexicography.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 678.4; specific comment within vv.20-24 block at DAR 678.4 on v.20
pioneerReadings.html: Smith titles vv.20-24 "Emotions of the Righteous" and gives v.20 a precise eschatological reason for the rejoicing: "The **apostles and prophets are here called upon to rejoice** over great Babylon in her destruction, as it is **in close connection with this destruction that they will all be delivered from the power of death and grave by the first resurrection**" (DAR 678.4). So for Smith the cause of rejoicing is not antiphonal symmetry to earth's lament, but the chronological adjacency of Babylon's fall to the saints' resurrection at the Second Coming.

Andrews provides the most precise pioneer cross-link from Rev 18:20 to Rev 19:1-9 (the heavenly Alleluia answering the call to rejoice): “while the saints eat the marriage supper, they behold the smoke of her burning; and in response to the call of Revelation 18:20, they rejoice over her. Revelation 19:1-9” (TMR 62.2). For Andrews the apostles and prophets called to rejoice are summoned to participate in the marriage-supper celebration of Rev 19, which itself is the heavenly answer to the long Rev 6:9-11 fifth-seal cry of the souls under the altar — though Andrews does not make the Rev 6 cross-link explicit here. The chronological anchor is firm: v.20 belongs to the moment during the seventh-plague execution of Babylon, not after the millennium.

Haskell reads verse 20 as the divine signal that the great-controversy verdict has been rendered. "None need partake of her plagues; for all had an opportunity to separate from her midst" (SSP 313.1) — the heavens and the apostles and the prophets rejoice because the long delay of vindication is finally over, and yet no soul has been lost who heard and heeded the call to come out (v.4). The "rejoicing" is therefore not vindictive but judicial-pastoral: the same God who labored to draw the saints out of Babylon now declares the case closed. Haskell sees in this verse the explicit prophetic fulfillment of every persecuted witness's prayer through the 1260 years and the loud-cry period; the church triumphant in heaven and the church militant on earth share the same verdict in the same hour.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 141
modernReadings.html: A structural hinge — the earthly lamentation (vv. 9-19) inverts into heavenly celebration; anticipates Rev 19:1-3. Verbatim: "The scene now changes and a voice exhorts the heavenly beings to celebrate the vengeance of God against Babylon… The theme of this verse will be picked up in Revelation 19:1-3" (p. 141).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule IV cross-link to Rev 19:1-3 is sound.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.20)
notes.html: The "antiphon to earth's lament / perfect moral inversion" reading is interpretive framing not in Smith's v.20 paragraph. The Rev 6:9-11 fifth-seal "how long" cross-link is standard pioneer typology but is not invoked here; Smith's anchor is the first resurrection, not the fifth-seal cry. The Rev 19:1-3 _Alleluia_ cross-link is also not in Smith's v.20 paragraph (he reserves the heavenly-chorus material for elsewhere).

verses.text: And a mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast it into the sea, saying, Thus with violence shall that great city Babylon be thrown down, and shall be found no more at all.

pioneerReadings.html: Ellen White treats verses 5-8 and 21 as a single judgment-tableau: Babylon, having filled the cup of her iniquity, is now ripe for the violent overthrow figured by the millstone hurled into the sea. The destruction is comprehensive and irreversible: "Such are the judgments that fall upon Babylon in the day of the visitation of God's wrath. She has filled up the measure of her iniquity; her time has come; she is ripe for destruction" (GC 653.3). White repeatedly quotes the language of verses 5-7, 11, 15-17, and 19 as direct prophecy of the post-probation collapse of the apostate religious-civil system, parallel to the seventh vial of Rev 16:19. She does not allegorize the millstone; she treats it as the prophetic emblem of a fall so sudden and final that the kings, merchants, and shipmasters can only watch and wail from a distance. The millstone falls under the same divine voice that turns the captivity of His people (GC 654.1).

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 679.1; specific comment within vv.20-24 block at DAR 679.1 on v.21
pioneerReadings.html: Smith's whole v.21 comment is a single sentence of irrevocable finality: "**Like a great millstone, Babylon sinks to rise no more**" (DAR 679.1). Earlier in his dramatic v.4 setup he had also written: "proud Babylon will go down as a millstone sinks in the depths of the sea" (DAR 672.1). The emphasis is finality, not OT typological re-enactment.

Litch's only sustained treatment of Babylon's final overthrow occurs in his exposition of the parallel passage in Revelation 16:17-21 (the seventh vial), which he equates with the millstone-cast of Rev 18:21: "under the seventh trumpet great Babylon entire will sink to rise no more at all" (PREX2 133.1). His structural reading of verses 17-21 of Rev 16 maps directly onto Rev 18:21 — the "great voice" from the temple saying "It is done," the world-shaking earthquake, the division of "the great city" (Rome) into three parts, the falling of the cities of the nations, and the bringing of "great Babylon" in remembrance to drink the wine of God's wrath (PREX1 183.3). Litch wrote before Bates and Andrews developed the two-stage fall doctrine, so he treats Babylon's fall and destruction as a single eschatological event under the seventh vial rather than the moral-fall-plus-literal-end framework that became standard in the Sabbatarian school.

Andrews uses verse 21 as decisive proof that Babylon in Revelation 18 cannot be a literal city and that her fall (Rev 18:2) must be sharply distinguished from her destruction (Rev 18:21). "Babylon is to be thrown down with violence, as a millstone is cast into the great deep, and she is to be utterly burned with fire. If this utter destruction were her ‘fall’, there would be no need of the second angel's proclamation to announce the fact; for her destruction is to be witnessed by the kings and merchants, and by every ship-master, and by all the company in ships, and by sailors, and by as many as trade by sea" (TMR 63.1). The two-stage reading hangs on this verse: the fall is a moral one preached before the plagues so God's people may escape; the millstone-cast is the literal end under the seventh vial (TMR 62.2). Without verse 21, the second angel's message collapses into the seventh plague and the call to come out becomes impossible.

Haskell closes his exposition of Revelation 18 by binding the millstone cast into the sea to the historical fall of ancient Babylon as a one-to-one type: "As the ancient city of Babylon was overthrown because she forsook the way of life, so modern Babylon dies. None need partake of her plagues; for all had an opportunity to separate from her midst" (SSP 313.1). For Haskell the violent throwing-down emphasizes the suddenness of the end and the irrevocability of the verdict; the "great controversy still continues; it ends in the overthrow of Babylon, the mother of harlots, and the confusion of Babel is replaced by the divine harmony, which, for six thousand years, has been marred by sin" (SSP 313.2). The millstone is, for Haskell, the prophetic answer to the long history of the persecuting power that began at Babel itself.

Bates's treatment of Babylon's structure provides the framework for verse 21: "She has harlot children, what do they represent, the Protestant and Greek Church, in all their organizations, represented in the 18 chap. The Angel says they were divided into three parts, 16:19" (BP2 64.1). The millstone falls not on Rome alone but on the entire three-fold harlot system — Roman, Greek, and Protestant — that constitutes Babylon. Bates explicitly links Rev 18:21 to Rev 16:19 (the great city divided into three parts) and to Rev 11:13 (a tenth part of the city fell in the French Revolution of 1790, which Bates reads as the first installment of the judgment). For Bates the millstone cast into the sea is the final and total execution of a judgment that began under the first French Revolution and has been advancing under each successive prophetic milestone.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 142
modernReadings.html: Typed directly from Jer 51:63-64 (Jeremiah's stone cast into the literal Euphrates); the stone = Babylon, drowned in the waters of the Euphrates (= peoples per Bohr's Rev 17:15 spiritualization). Verbatim: "The stone here is a metaphor for Babylon. She will be drowned in the waters of the Euphrates" (p. 142).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation on the Jer 51 type/antitype trace itself (Rule XII applied correctly). _However_, the secondary statement "drowned in the waters of the Euphrates" implicitly invokes Bohr's Euphrates = peoples-of-the-world spiritualization (Catalog A), which carries the Rev 16:12 / Rev 9:14 Rule-V violation forward into Rev 18:21. The Jer 51 anchor in the verse itself is sound; the cross-link to Euphrates depends on framework.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.21)
notes.html: The Jer 51:63-64 type/antitype trace (scroll bound to stone cast into Euphrates) is standard pioneer typology but is not invoked in Smith's v.21 paragraph; Smith never quotes Jer 51:63-64 in his Rev 18 commentary. The "sea = peoples of the world per Rev 17:15" cross-link is also not in Smith's v.21 paragraph. Smith's reading is simply: Babylon sinks, never to rise.

verses.text: And the voice of harpers, and musicians, and of pipers, and trumpeters, shall be heard no more at all in thee; and no craftsman, of whatsoever craft he be, shall be found any more in thee; and the sound of a millstone shall be heard no more at all in thee.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 679.1; specific comment within vv.20-24 block at DAR 679.1 on v.22
pioneerReadings.html: Smith gives the music-craft-millstone cessation a single sentence: "**The various arts and crafts that have been employed in her midst, and have ministered to her desires, shall be practiced no more. The pompous music that has been employed in her imposing but formal and lifeless service, dies away forever**" (DAR 679.1). Notice Smith's specific application: the music that ceases is "**her imposing but formal and lifeless service**" — i.e. the ceremonial music of the apostate religious system, not generic civilizational fine arts.

For Haskell verse 22 is the prophetic fulfillment of Amos 8:11-12: "Behold, the days come, saith the Lord God, that I will send a famine in the land, not a famine of bread, nor a thirst for water, but of hearing the words of the Lord" (SSP 312.1). The silencing of harpers, musicians, pipers, trumpeters, and craftsmen pictures the total cessation of Babylon's internal life once probation has closed. The judgment is not mere desolation; it is the withdrawal of every social occupation by which Babylon once held humanity. Haskell ties this directly to the post-probation experience pictured in EW 282: the wicked who once filled the streets with music and craft now suffer agony, while the saints, hidden in the wilderness, are fed by angels. The voice of joy is turned to mourning because the bridegroom is no longer in her midst.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 142
modernReadings.html: No discrete verse-22 exegesis; treated under "Cessation of City Life" (vv. 21-24) — all city activities cease when Babylon falls. Verbatim: "All the activities that characterize a city will cease when Babylon falls (verses 22, 23)" (p. 142).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule XII trace to Jer 25:10 is exact and confirms the literal-Babylon-type / spiritual-Babylon-antitype symmetry.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.22)
notes.html: The Jer 25:10 type/antitype trace is standard pioneer typology but is not invoked in Smith's v.22 paragraph. The "Babylon as civilization, not merely religious system" generalization runs against Smith's narrower reading — Smith specifically reads the silenced music as the **liturgical music of formal religion** (the very thing that has "ministered to her desires" in worship contexts).

verses.text: And the light of a candle shall shine no more at all in thee; and the voice of the bridegroom and of the bride shall be heard no more at all in thee: for thy merchants were the great men of the earth; for by thy sorceries were all nations deceived.

pioneerReadings.html: Ellen White singles out verses 22-23 as the verses every reader should study most carefully: "Take each verse of this chapter, and read it carefully, especially the last two: ‘And the light of a candle shall shine no more at all in thee; and the voice of the bridegroom and of the bride shall be heard no more at all in thee: for thy merchants were the great men of the earth; for by thy sorceries were all nations deceived’" (16MR 270.2). She binds verse 23 directly to the loud-cry call of verses 2-5 (16MR 270.1) and identifies the sorceries with the spiritualistic miracles by which Satan personates Christ in the final delusion (GC 624.2). The "merchants who were the great men of the earth" are read as the ecclesiastical-commercial union of apostate Protestantism with civil power that constitutes Babylon herself; the candle going out marks the close of probation in her midst.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 679.1; 679.2; specific comment within vv.20-24 block at DAR 679.1-2 on v.23
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads the candle and bridegroom/bride clause as concrete domestic cessation: "**The scenes of festivity and gladness, when the bridegroom and the bride have been led before her altars, shall be witnessed no more**" (DAR 679.1) — again the application is religious-ceremonial (weddings led to her altars), not generic domestic life. On the sorceries clause, Smith is unambiguous and concrete: "**Her sorceries constitute her leading crime; and sorcery is a practice which is involved in the Spiritualism of to-day**" (DAR 679.2). So Smith identifies Babylon's principal _pharmakeía_ as **Spiritualism specifically** (consistent with his earlier v.2 anchor at DAR 667.1 — Spiritualism is the agency completing the demonic-possession of fallen Babylon).

Haskell threads verses 22-23 together as the picture of Babylon after probation closes: "There is no word of God in Babylon" (SSP 312.2). The candle no longer shines because the Spirit has withdrawn; the bridegroom's voice is silent because Christ has ceased His mediation; the merchants who were "great men of the earth" represent the corporations, trusts, and ecclesiastical-commercial powers Haskell had already identified in SSP 308.1. The sorceries by which all nations are deceived are the spiritualistic miracles attending the image to the beast and the personation of Christ (SSP 309.1-2). For Haskell, verse 23 is not future poetry but a present trajectory: every silenced sound and extinguished candle traces back to choices Babylon has already been making during the loud cry.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 142-143
modernReadings.html: Reads _pharmakeía_ (sorcery) as a spiritual narcotic — magic, drugs, casting of spells — by which Babylon deceives the nations into incoherence; lexically traced through Rev 9:21; 21:8; 22:15. Verbatim: "Those who visit Babylon's pharmacy are not able to think straight!" (p. 142).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. The _pharmakeía_ lexical observation is sound (the same word appears in Rev 9:21, 21:8, 22:15 — Rule XII trace within Revelation itself).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.23)
notes.html: The "five-fold seal of permanence" cataloguing of "no more at all" across vv.21-23 is structural overlay. The _pharmakeía_ lexical chain through Rev 9:21; 21:8; 22:15 is sound Rule-XII tracing but is not made in Smith's v.23 paragraph. The "Spiritualism + Mariolatry + the mass" triad expands Smith — DAR 679.2 names only **Spiritualism** as the present-day instantiation; the natural-immortality/mass/saint-veneration framework is from Smith's separate treatment at DAR 487.2-487.4 / 667.2, not from his v.23 paragraph.

verses.text: And in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth.

pioneerReadings.html: Ellen White treats verse 24 as the divine vindication of every martyr from Abel to the last witness slain under the image of the beast. In her great-controversy framework the blood found in Babylon includes the Albigenses, the Waldenses, the Reformers, the Puritan dissenters, and every saint persecuted by the apostate religious-civil system in any age (cf. GC chs. 4-15, summarized at 654.1). The judgment scene in GC 654.1-4 directly answers verse 24: "the rich prided themselves upon their superiority to those who were less favored; but they had obtained their riches by violation of the law of God. They had neglected to feed the hungry, to clothe the naked, to deal justly, and to love mercy" — the same indictment Revelation places on Babylon herself. For White, verse 24 explains why the millstone falls: Babylon's judgment is not arbitrary; she is shown to have shed all the blood of God's witnesses by directly killing some and by aiding-and-abetting the dominion under which the rest died.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 679.2; specific comment within vv.20-24 block at DAR 679.2 on v.24
pioneerReadings.html: Smith's whole v.24 comment makes two precise moves: (a) "**'And in her was found the blood' of 'all that were slain upon the earth.' From this it is evident that ever since the introduction of a false religion into the world, Babylon has existed**. In her has been found, all along, opposition to the work of God, and persecution of his people"; and (b) for "the guilt of the last generation, see on chapter 16:6" (DAR 679.2). So Smith expands "Babylon" diachronically all the way back to the **introduction of false religion** (not merely the 1260-year medieval papal persecution), and cross-refers his own Rev 16:6 commentary for the eschatological blood-guilt's specifically last-generation charge.

Andrews points to verse 24 as proof that Babylon is the entire apostate religious system across all her divisions, not just papal Rome: the blood-guilt charge sweeps in Protestant complicity as well. "The Protestant Church, till within a short time, held many thousand slaves; nor is the fact to be disguised that the professed church was the right arm of the slave power" (TMR 45.1). For Andrews this verse forces the reader to acknowledge that Babylon's daughters (Rev 17:5) share the mother's blood-guilt; the slave-power complicity of the American churches is, on his reading, a literal nineteenth-century instance of Babylon being "found" with the blood of saints. The verse therefore stands as a moral-historical warrant for the second angel's message (Rev 14:8) being preached against Protestant bodies and not against Rome alone (cf. TMR 64.1).

Haskell quotes verse 24 verbatim within his exposition of Babylon's end: "And in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth" (SSP 312.2). He reads it as the explanation for the candle going out and the bridegroom going silent — Babylon's judgment is the long-deferred answer to her persecuting history. The "two witnesses" silenced "for forty and two months" (Rev 11:2-3) and the blood of all the martyrs of the 1260-year period are found in her at the close, just as the blood of Abel cried from the ground to indict Cain. For Haskell this verse closes the great-controversy arc: the same apostate system that persecuted the church through the wilderness period (Rev 12:6, 14) is now arraigned with comprehensive blood-guilt, and her destruction in verse 21 follows as judicial necessity.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 143
modernReadings.html: The climactic indictment — blood-guilt as the defining mark of Babylon, found in every antichrist passage (Matt 24; Dan 7, 8, 11; Rev 13, 17, 18). Verbatim: "The great sin of Babylon is that she persecutes God's people and sheds their blood. This is the picture that we find in every one of the antichrist passages" (p. 143).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule IV (bringing all antichrist-blood-guilt passages together). Note: the Dan 11:44-45 cross-link depends on Bohr's spiritualized Dan 11 reading, which fails Rule XI elsewhere — but the _blood-guilt_ identification at Rev 18:24 is rule-compliant on its own.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at v.24)
notes.html: The Matt 23:35 ("Abel onward") cross-link is standard pioneer/EGW typology but is not invoked in Smith's v.24 paragraph; Smith's diachronic move is "ever since the introduction of a false religion," not specifically Matt 23:35's "from Abel." The Rev 19:1-3 _Alleluia_ "doxology answer to centuries of unanswered blood" connection is not in Smith's v.24 paragraph; Smith refers the reader back to his own Rev 16:6 treatment, not forward to Rev 19.

summaryHtml:
**Rev 18 violation summary:** Of 24 verses, Bohr has Miller-rule violations on **1** (v. 2 — the collapse of Smith's two-stage Rev 14:8 / Rev 18:2 framing into a single terminal moral fall, weakening Rule IV). The previously flagged v. 13 "slaves" violation is **withdrawn after DAR audit**: Smith himself spiritualizes "slaves and souls of men" as creedal slavery-of-conscience (DAR 676.2), so Bohr's body/soul-ontology reading is in the same hermeneutic register as the DAR pioneer reading, not in violation of it. (A literalist Rule-XI critique against any spiritualization of "slaves" is exegetically defensible but is not the DAR pioneer baseline.) **This is Bohr's most rule-compliant chapter** in the entire contested corpus. The "Babylonian Capitalism" thesis is original contemporary application, not symbol redefinition.
